content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission


September 4, 1991


James R. Sutton
Nielsen, Merksamer, Hodgson,
Parrinello & Mueller
591 Redwood Highway, #4000
Mill Valley, CA  94941

Re:  Your Request For Informal
     Assistance; Our File
     No. I-91-347

Dear Mr. Sutton:

     You have requested confirmation of your understanding of
Commission advice under the lobbying disclosure provisions and the
conflict of interest disclosure provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").1/  Because you have requested general
guidelines, and are not asking for advice concerning an identified
person who has disclosure duties under the Act, we treat your
letter as one for informal assistance, rather than formal
advice.2/

     Your question concerns the method of determining the value of
food and drink received by an official at a "drop-in" reception or
similar event with an open bar and self-serve food table.

     Please find enclosed the following Commission advice letters
which discuss the method for determining the value of such gifts:


_________________________
1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to
Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/  Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800  •  P.O. Box 807  •  Sacramento CA 95804-0807  •  (916)322-5660

James R. Sutton
Page Two


Berman Advice Letter, No. A-81-099 (maintaining list of names
of guests at a function)

Eichman Advice Letter, No. A-84-098 (various expenses
and whether each is included in determining
the total cost of an event)

Blonien Advice Letter, No. I-89-490 (speaker fees are
included in total cost of an event)

File Memo No. M-91-273 (travel costs paid to speaker are
included in total cost of an event)

You will note from the Commission's previous advice that
payments for room rental, decorations, entertainment, speakers,
and certain other expenses are included in determining the total
cost, and the pro rata value of attendance, at an event. However,
when an official merely drops in at an event for a short period of
time and does not stay and enjoy the entertainment or partake of
an average amount of food and beverage, it would not appear to be
necessary to allocate a portion of those expenses to the amount of
the gift received by the official. Rather, it seems reasonable to
attribute only the actual value of the drink or other item
consumed by the official.

As you pointed out in your letter, it is crucial that
detailed, accurate records are kept in order to comply with the
Act's disclosure requirements and limitations with regard to gifts
to officials.

If you have any questions about this letter, please call me
at (916) 322-5662.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

[illegible]

By: Jeanne Pritchard
Chief, Technical Assistance
and Analysis Division
